title: Case study: Small business faced big health insurance hike
summary: Amy Bielawski, a 61-year-old Georgia entrepreneur who has run her own entertainment company for 32 years, dropped her ACA marketplace health insurance because out-of-pocket costs became unaffordable. The article is part of STAT's 'Out of Pocket, Out of Reach' series examining how soaring healthcare costs are reshaping the labor market for small business owners.
analysis_notes: Full article content behind STAT+ paywall. Only the first couple paragraphs were visible before the paywall error message appeared repeatedly. Summary inferred from available text, image alt text, and related article links in the recommended section.

title: Compass says depression drug has long-lasting benefits
summary: Vertex Pharma announced its largest-ever acquisition, a $10 billion deal to buy Crinetics Pharmaceuticals, which develops drugs for rare endocrine disorders. Separately, Compass Therapeutics reported that its psilocybin-based depression treatment demonstrated long-lasting therapeutic benefits. The newsletter also highlights rising health insurance costs affecting Americans.
analysis_notes: Article body is locked behind STAT+ paywall. Summary derived from visible headline, lead paragraph (first sentence about Vertex/Crinetics $10B deal), and newsletter metadata in email share text. The Compass psilocybin readout details were not accessible — content quality for that story is weak. The Vertex-Crinetics deal was confirmed by a separate non-paywalled article on the same topic (2026/07/06): 'Vertex acquires Crinetics Pharmaceuticals for $10 billion as biotech M&A booms'.

title: What if this Ebola outbreak can't be stopped?
summary: Opinion piece arguing that the greatest risk in the current Ebola outbreak in eastern DRC is not spread to other countries, but rather it becoming permanently endemic and burning for decades without sustained international cooperation and local infrastructure. The authors, drawing on their experience from the 2014–2016 West Africa epidemic and the 2018–2020 DRC outbreaks, warn that the conditions that ended the last epidemics — extraordinary international cooperation, local adaptations, and luck — are largely absent today.
analysis_notes: Article is behind a STAT paywall; full body text was not accessible. Analysis based on headline, lead paragraphs, email preview text ('If the Ebola outbreak isn't extinguished, it could burn for decades, posing an ongoing threat to global health.'), author credentials, and metadata. Two of three topic tags (global health, infectious diseases) visible in markup; policy tag also present.

title: This whistleblower took on a health insurance giant and a political machine. She's not stopping there
summary: Chris Deacon, an attorney and health insurance consultant who served as the top official overseeing New Jersey’s state health plan, was instrumental in securing a $100 million fraud settlement against Horizon Blue Cross Blue Shield for knowingly overpaying hospitals and doctors while fraudulently winning its state contract. Despite her pivotal role, Deacon felt disgusted at a press conference where officials erased her contributions and glossed over their own culpability in allowing the misconduct to persist. This article is part of STAT’s “Out of Pocket, Out of Reach” series examining employer-based health insurance costs.
analysis_notes: Article was behind STAT+ paywall; only the opening ~2 paragraphs were accessible. The headline, author byline, image caption (identifying Deacon as attorney/consultant who played crucial role in NJ state workers' $100M fraud settlement), and sidebar metadata provided sufficient context to construct a useful summary. The article is part three of STAT's "Out of Pocket, Out of Reach" series on employer-based insurance whistleblower stories.

title: Ebola outbreak exposes a fractured outbreak response system
summary: STAT Morning Rounds roundup by Allison DeAngelis and Helen Branswell covering multiple health stories, led by coverage of the Ebola outbreak in Congo and Uganda as evidence of a broken global outbreak response infrastructure. Includes reporting on employer-based health insurance costs driving small businesses to abandon coverage, analysis of Gen Z doctors and changing medical attitudes, and broader concerns about healthcare affordability. Full article text was behind a STAT+ paywall.

title: The crumbling employer-based health insurance system
summary: This D.C. Diagnosis newsletter entry covers the growing crisis in employer-based health insurance, drawing on Bob Herman's "Out of Pocket, Out of Reach" series examining soaring out-of-pocket health costs for American workers. The piece touches on clinical trial diversity and 340b drug pricing alongside troubling trends in the employer-sponsored insurance market.
analysis_notes: Article is behind a paywall; actual body content was not accessible. This summary and analysis are based on surrounding metadata, newsletter context, linked article titles in the sidebar (small business health insurance, employer-based insurance whistleblower), topic tags, and brief introductory text visible above the paywall wall.

title: "Out of Pocket, Out of Reach" — America's Employer Health Insurance Crisis
summary: STAT's Bob Herman launches a special series called 'Out of Pocket, Out of Reach' examining the crumbling state of America's employer-based health insurance system. A companion preview piece in this week's Health Care Inc. newsletter teases that U.S. workers and businesses are facing a crisis where health insurance costs are rising far faster than inflation, with earnings quietly suffering behind the scenes.
analysis_notes: Full article text is behind a STAT+ paywall; only the newsletter intro, teaser snippet, title, and related links were visible. The piece is a series launch/introductions rather than a data-heavy investigation.

title: ‘Secret shopper’ study probes GLP-1 telehealth sites
summary: This article, by Mario Aguilar, is from STAT's Health Tech newsletter and covers a 'secret shopper' study examining telehealth sites that prescribe GLP-1 medications. Based on metadata context, the study investigated how these online platforms dispense weight-loss drugs with varying levels of clinical oversight. The same edition also covers news about Epic, Anthropic, and updates on brain implants.
analysis_notes: Content is behind STAT+ paywall — only newsletter summary text and metadata accessible. A related article referenced in 'Subscriber Picks' provides more detail: 'Online GLP-1 prescriptions are often fast, easy — and low on clinical oversight' (JAMA/yale study, July 6). Summary derived from surrounding article list context rather than full text.

title: Why America's small businesses are giving up on health insurance
summary: Part 1 of STAT's 'Out of Pocket, Out of Reach' series examines how America's small businesses are dropping employer-sponsored health insurance at the lowest rate ever, driven by skyrocketing premiums for hospitals, doctors, and prescription drugs. The percentage of working-age adults getting coverage through jobs has fallen from 67% in 1998 to about 60%, with smaller shops bearing the brunt of rising costs that eat into bottom lines and paychecks.
analysis_notes: Article content is partially behind STAT+ paywall. Summary derived from the freely available lead section and surrounding context (author bio, series description, recommended articles). Full article body inaccessible; findings are limited to the opening paragraphs which provide a solid overview of trends and drivers.

title: FDA approves Vera Therapeutics kidney disease treatment
summary: The FDA has approved Trutakna, a new medicine from biotech company Vera Therapeutics, for the treatment of IgA nephropathy (IgAN)—a chronic autoimmune kidney disease caused by harmful antibody buildup that leads to progressive kidney dysfunction and can result in organ failure requiring dialysis. CEO Marshall Fordyce expressed excitement about bringing the drug to patients in an interview ahead of the announcement.
analysis_notes: Article content is thin—only the first two paragraphs were visible before hitting a STAT+ paywall. The snippet email body included additional context about IgAN being of wide biotech interest. No clinical data, trial results, or pricing information was accessible without subscription.

title: 5 takeaways from STAT's series on the soaring cost of health insurance
summary: Bob Herman summarizes insights from the 'Out of Pocket, Out of Reach' series examining why employer-based health insurance is becoming unaffordable for millions of Americans. Key finding: fewer than 60% of small firms with 200 or fewer workers now offer health insurance, reaching an all-time low as costs push businesses to a financial cliff. Small businesses face deductibles more than 50% higher than larger firms and risk unexpected surcharges from insurers if enrollment rises.
analysis_notes: Full article content is behind STAT+ paywall. Analysis based on available excerpts, topic tags, and related article links visible on the page.

title: Pharmalittle: We're reading about a $10 billion Vertex deal, the pace of FDA drug approvals, and more
summary: Vertex Pharmaceuticals will pay $10 billion to acquire Crinetics Pharmaceuticals, acquiring its approved drug Palsonify for treating acromegaly (a rare endocrine disorder) launched last year, as well as late-stage drug candidates including a therapy for congenital adrenal hyperplasia. The deal is the largest in Vertex's history as the Boston-based company builds out products beyond its cystic fibrosis portfolio. This STAT+ exclusive also covers telehealth-era concerns about GLP-1 obesity drug prescribing practices and notes that FDA drug approvals have maintained their pace despite industry job cuts.
analysis_notes: Article is behind a STAT+ paywall. Only the lede and first paragraph about Vertex/Crinetics deal are visible. The article title also references FDA approval pace, but the full content on that topic (and any GLP-1 telehealth prescribing details) was not accessible in the markdown provided.

title: What a chat with Anthropic's CEO told me about how AI may affect biotech
summary: Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei acknowledged that his earlier vision of AI delivering a decade's worth of biotech progress per year is not yet realistic, citing underdeveloped model capabilities, researcher adoption timelines, and lagging infrastructure and regulatory systems. The admission came at an Anthropic event where the company unveiled Claude Science, a product aimed at biologists and pharmaceutical companies.
analysis_notes: Full article text is behind STAT+ paywall. Analysis based on the first ~300 words of free preview plus article metadata, topic tags, and references to related articles (e.g., Claude Science release, Anthropic drug development plans). Key themes extracted from visible content: Amodei tempering hype around AI-accelerated biotech timelines, the launch of Claude Science product for scientists/pharma.

title: Four major biotech updates to catch up on
summary: STAT's Readout newsletter covers four key developments: Novartis acquires U.K.-based Myricx Bio for $1.1 billion upfront plus up to $400 million in milestones, gaining an antibody-drug conjugate platform targeting solid tumors; Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei tempers expectations about AI transforming biotech, acknowledging biology's inherent complexity and long timelines; Medicare proposes steep cuts to 340B drug payments; and GOP lawmakers move to preserve diversity protections in clinical trials while former Surgeon General Jerome Adams weighs in on peptide regulation. The article is partially behind a paywall, with full details on the Anthropic interview and other stories inaccessible.
analysis_notes: Article is a newsletter roundup behind STAT+ paywall. Only the lead paragraph and Novartis/Myricx deal details are visible as free content; the Anthropic CEO interview, Medicare 340B cuts, clinical trial diversity, and peptide regulation stories are truncated to headlines only. Priority is medium because the one fully visible story (Novartis acquisition of Myricx) appears significant in drug development, but the broader context about AI in biotech and policy changes cannot be verified from available content.

title: Ebola vaccine, marathon wall, mental health: Morning Rounds
summary: STAT's Morning Rounds newsletter covers the race to develop Ebola therapies amid an outbreak exposing a fractured response system, male marathoners who appear to be more likely to 'hit the wall' during races, and 2026 mental health trends. GOP lawmakers are also pushing for greater diversity in clinical trials after the Trump administration rolled back prior diversity policies.
analysis_notes: Full article text was behind STAT+ paywall ('Switch out of private browsing or subscribe to STAT+' error). Summary and entity extraction rely only on headline, section teasers, author bylines, and sidebar links. Content quality is limited due to paywall — actual findings, therapy names, data points, and specific study details are unavailable.
